text: The metrics have been moving against the baseline, and four of five are on track.
The change works. Now the board asks: which investment delivers the most value?

My recommendation is to buy. The ROI is the highest of the two options, and the payback is quicker.
But buying has the higher upfront cost. Why not build, if it is cheaper?
Building is cheaper to start, but buying is more cost-effective over three years. It is also more scalable.
And the downside of buying? I do not want only the good side.
The downside is the price tag. But it is the most feasible path, so we should allocate the budget to buy.
Good. Show me the payback as one number, and send me the updated business case by Wednesday.
The downside is the upfront cost, but the payback is quicker.
We should allocate the budget to the most cost-effective option.

Carlos, before the board asks you to justify the spend, let me brief you on the two options for the next phase. Option one is to build the platform in-house. The upfront cost is lower, and it is more flexible than a vendor product, because the team can shape it. But the payback period is longer, the lead time is the biggest risk, and it is harder to scale. Option two is to buy from the vendor. It is more expensive upfront, but it is faster, it is more scalable, and the return on investment is higher over three years, because the payback is quicker. So the honest comparison is this: building is cheaper to start but slower to pay back; buying costs more now but delivers the most value over the cycle. The downside of building is the time; the downside of buying is the price tag the board will see first. My advice: lead with the option that is the most cost-effective over three years, not the one that is the cheapest on day one. Show the numbers side by side, name the trade-off, and recommend the one that is more viable for where we want to be. Boards approve the spend when the case is clear, not when it is loud.

Hi Carlos, this is Diana, with a quick recap after the investment review. The directors liked the way you made the case. You compared the two options side by side, you were clear that buying is more expensive upfront but more cost-effective over three years, and you did not hide the downside of either path, which they respected. A few notes for next time. First, the payback period: that was your strongest number, so lead with it, because a shorter payback is the most persuasive point for this board. Second, scalability: you said the vendor option is more scalable, but the board wanted proof, so bring one concrete example of how it scales. Third, the upfront cost: do not apologize for it; frame it as the price of the higher return. One thing to add: a single slide that shows the return on investment of each option as one clear number, because the board decides faster when the comparison is one line, not ten. Send me the updated business case by Wednesday, and we will walk the lead director through it before the vote. Strong work, Carlos. You made the more expensive option sound like the smarter one, and that is exactly the case we needed. Talk soon.

First, state the recommendation. Then compare the two options side by side. Next, lead with the strongest number, the payback. After that, name the downside of each option honestly. Finally, ask the board to allocate the budget.
Buying is more cost-effective than building over three years.
The downside is the upfront cost, but the payback is quicker.
